title: Sorted Squares Rebuilder
description:
Given a nondecreasing array, return a new array of the squares of each number, also in nondecreasing order. Use two pointers to fill from the end since large absolute values sit at the extremes.

    

Constraints:
    - 1 ≤ len(nums) ≤ 10^5
- -10^4 ≤ nums[i] ≤ 10^4
explanation:
Intuition
The largest square at any time comes from the larger absolute value at either end. Place that into the output from right to left.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Allocate result array.
2. Main Algorithm: Use i and j at the ends and a k pointer at the end of output. Pick the larger square and move that side inward.
3. Return Result: Return the filled output.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) because the scan adjusts pointers a limited
  number of times and avoids nested loops.
- Space Complexity: O(n) since only a few variables or an output
  list are used.

Key Insights
- Fill from the back to avoid extra passes
- Absolute values guide which pointer to move

Common Pitfalls
- Forgetting to move k after placement
- Computing square twice without storing can be slower
solutionCode:
def sorted_squares_rebuilder(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    res = [0] * n
    i, j = 0, n - 1
    k = n - 1
    while i <= j:
        left = nums[i] * nums[i]
        right = nums[j] * nums[j]
        if left > right:
            res[k] = left
            i += 1
        else:
            res[k] = right
            j -= 1
        k -= 1
    return res

testCases.explanation: Two pointer fill from the back

hints: The largest square sits at one extreme
Write from right to left into the output
Move the pointer that produced the placed square
